title: API key hardcoded in source code
description: Detects API keys and secrets hardcoded directly in mobile source files. These credentials are easily extractable through reverse engineering or repository access, enabling unauthorized API access and potential data breaches.
remediation: Move API keys to secure platform storage (Keychain on iOS, EncryptedSharedPreferences on Android) or use environment-based configuration injected at build time.

title: Token stored in SharedPreferences without encryption
description: Detects storage of authentication tokens and credentials in Android SharedPreferences without encryption. SharedPreferences stores data in plaintext XML files at /data/data/<package>/shared_prefs/, accessible on rooted devices or through backups.
remediation: Replace SharedPreferences with EncryptedSharedPreferences from the AndroidX Security library, which encrypts both keys and values using AES-256-GCM.

title: Credentials stored in NSUserDefaults
description: Detects storage of sensitive credentials in iOS NSUserDefaults. UserDefaults stores data in an unencrypted plist file within the app sandbox, which can be accessed through device backups, forensic tools, or on jailbroken devices.
remediation: Use iOS Keychain Services (SecItemAdd/SecItemCopyMatching) or a wrapper library like KeychainAccess to store credentials with hardware-backed encryption.

title: Sensitive data written to unencrypted file
description: Detects writing of sensitive data such as passwords, tokens, or secrets to unencrypted files on the filesystem. These files can be accessed by other apps on rooted/jailbroken devices or extracted through backups and forensic analysis.
remediation: Use platform-specific encrypted file APIs (EncryptedFile on Android, Data Protection with NSFileProtectionComplete on iOS) or encrypt data before writing to disk.

title: Credentials logged to console output
description: Detects logging of sensitive credentials to console output. On Android, log output is readable by other apps with READ_LOGS permission and persists in logcat. On iOS, logs may be captured in device crash reports and syslog. Logged credentials may be captured by analytics SDKs or crash reporters.
remediation: Never log sensitive data. Implement a logging wrapper that automatically redacts fields matching sensitive patterns. Use ProGuard/R8 rules to strip Log calls from release builds on Android.
